edge: Vision transformers and post-training quantization are closely related as the high training cost of vision transformers necessitates exploring post-training quantization methods to improve efficiency.
NoisyQuant is proposed as a quantizer-agnostic enhancement for post-training activation quantization performance specifically tailored for vision transformers.

NoisyQuant enhances the post-training activation quantization performance by adding a fixed Uniform noisy bias to the values being quantized, effectively reducing quantization error under provable conditions.

NoisyQuant actively alters the heavy-tailed activation distribution of vision transformers with additive noisy bias to fit a given quantizer, leading to significant improvement in post-training quantization performance.
NoisyQuant largely improves the post-training quantization performance of vision transformers with minimal computation overhead, showcasing its effectiveness.

Improved performance of vision transformers with NoisyQuant is evident from the significant enhancement in top-1 accuracy on ImageNet, surpassing previous nonlinear and mixed-precision quantization methods.
NoisyQuant improves SOTA top-1 accuracy on ImageNet by up to 1.7%, 1.1% and 0.5% for ViT, DeiT, and Swin Transformer respectively, showcasing its superior performance in post-training quantization.

The difficulty of post-training vision transformer activation quantization due to heavy-tailed activation distribution is aimed to be resolved.
Post-training quantization (PTQ) is a preferable method in scenarios with limited training data or computational resources for expensive quantization-aware fine-tuning.
Previous methods have studied PTQ on CNNs, while this work focuses on vision transformers.
EasyQuant presents a quick searching mechanism to determine proper clipping range for quantization.
ZeroQ proposes a distillation mechanism using proxy input images to conduct PTQ leveraging batch normalization layers' statistics.
SQuant calibrates the model for lower quantization error based on sensitivity obtained from the Hessian spectrum.
The concept of lower quantization error is derived from the goal of reducing the quantization error in the context.
The sensitivity is obtained based on the Hessian spectrum in the context.
A linear PTQ method for vision transformers uses Pearson correlation coefficients to determine scaling factors according to Liu et al.

PTQ4ViT introduces a nonlinear Twin Uniform Quantization method for vision transformers' activation distributions, which involves setting different scaling factors based on positive and negative activations of GeLU, and small and large values of Softmax.
By differentiating scaling factors based on small and large values of Softmax, the method aims to reduce the activation quantization error to some extent.
[19, 34] reparameterize the LN layer to suppress outliers by scaling down activation values.

Unlike previous PTQ methods that analyze the activation distribution and fit the quantizer accordingly, the proposed method involves actively modifying the distribution being quantized with a sampled noisy bias.
The proposed method aims to bring quantization error reduction and performance enhancement for all the PTQ quantizers for vision transformer activation quantization.
